title: Lifestyle English Practice Pack
theme: Lifestyle: travel, routines, wellness, food, work-life balance, and everyday choices
platform_note: Original MET-inspired practice materials for platform use. These are not official MET items.

reading.section_note: Ten short lifestyle/travel-related passages progressing from B1+ to B2. Questions include skimming, scanning, detail, vocabulary, and inference.

exercises.title: A Calm Check-In

image_generation_prompt.purpose: Visual prompt for AI image generation connected to Reading Exercise 1.
image_generation_prompt.prompt_en: A realistic, detailed illustration of a traveler checking in at a small modern hotel reception desk. The traveler is carrying a small suitcase and a backpack, looking tired but relieved. A friendly receptionist is handing over a key card and pointing to a simple city map on the counter. In the background, there is a comfortable lobby with plants, a sofa, soft warm lighting, and a clock on the wall. No visible brand names, no logos, no readable text. Clean international setting, natural colors, clear actions, uncluttered composition, suitable for an English speaking or reading exercise, 16:9 horizontal format.
image_generation_prompt.negative_prompt: No logos, no brand names, no readable text, no distorted hands, no extra limbs, no blurry faces, no watermark, no crowded scene, no luxury exaggeration, no unrealistic hotel signs.

exercises.passage: Maya arrived at the hotel just after 9 p.m. after a long day of travel. She had planned to go straight to bed, but the receptionist explained that the hotel café was still open for another thirty minutes. Maya asked for a quiet room because she had an online meeting early the next morning. The receptionist checked the system and moved her from a room near the elevator to one on the fourth floor, facing the garden. Before going upstairs, Maya bought a sandwich and a bottle of water from the café. She felt tired, but she was relieved that the first evening of her trip had become easier than expected.

questions.question: Why did Maya ask for a quiet room?
options: She had an early online meeting.

questions.question: The hotel café was already closed when Maya arrived.
What did the receptionist do to help Maya?
How did Maya probably feel at the end of the passage?
Where was Maya’s new room facing?

exercises.teaching_note: Use this as a gentle first reading task. Ask students to underline time references and hotel-service vocabulary.

exercises.title: A Review of a Weekend Apartment
exercises.passage: We stayed in a small apartment near the river for two nights. The location was excellent because we could walk to cafés, museums, and a weekend market. The apartment was clean and bright, although the kitchen was smaller than it looked in the photos. The host sent clear instructions before we arrived, so check-in was simple. At night, the street was a little noisy, especially on Saturday, but closing the windows helped. I would recommend this place to travelers who want to explore the city on foot and do not mind a lively neighborhood.

questions.question: What is the main purpose of the text?
The reviewer says the kitchen was bigger than expected.
Who would probably enjoy this apartment most?
options: Travelers who want a quiet countryside stay
Travelers who like walking around the city
People who need a large kitchen

questions.question: The reviewer’s overall opinion is...

exercises.teaching_note: Good for distinguishing main idea from supporting details.

exercises.title: A Health Retreat in the Mountains
exercises.passage: The three-day mountain retreat is designed for people who want a short break from busy routines. Each morning begins with light stretching and a guided walk through the forest. After breakfast, guests can join workshops on stress management, healthy cooking, or sleep habits. The program is flexible, so visitors may skip any activity and spend time reading or resting instead. Meals are included and are made with local vegetables, grains, and fresh fruit. The retreat is not a medical treatment, but many guests say they leave with more energy and a clearer plan for improving their daily lifestyle.

questions.question: What is the retreat mainly for?
options: Taking a break from busy routines

questions.question: Guests must attend every activity.
Which activity is mentioned in the passage?
In the text, 'flexible' means...

exercises.teaching_note: Follow up with a speaking question: Would you join this retreat? Why?

exercises.title: Airport Wellness Announcement
exercises.passage: Attention passengers. During today’s delays, the airport is offering free access to the Quiet Zone on Level 2, near Gate 18. This area includes comfortable seats, charging points, and low lighting. Passengers can also join a ten-minute stretching session at 3:00 p.m. and 5:00 p.m. near the information desk. Please remember that food is not allowed inside the Quiet Zone, but bottled water is permitted. Families with small children may prefer the play area opposite Gate 12.

questions.question: Why is the airport offering the Quiet Zone?
Where is the Quiet Zone?
Passengers can take bottled water into the Quiet Zone.
What may families with small children prefer?

exercises.teaching_note: Practice fast scanning: give students 60 seconds to find times and places.

exercises.title: A Smart Lunch Menu
exercises.passage: Green Plate Café offers three lunch sets from Monday to Friday. The Energy Bowl includes brown rice, grilled chicken, avocado, and vegetables. The Garden Plate is vegetarian and comes with lentils, roasted carrots, salad, and yogurt sauce. The Quick Soup Set includes vegetable soup, whole-grain bread, and a small fruit cup. Customers who bring a reusable cup receive a discount on coffee or tea. The café is busiest between 12:30 and 1:15, so visitors who want a quieter lunch should arrive before noon or after 1:30.

questions.question: Which option is vegetarian?
Customers can get a discount if they bring a reusable cup.
What time is best for a quieter lunch?
What comes with the Quick Soup Set?

exercises.teaching_note: Useful for food vocabulary and making lifestyle recommendations.

exercises.title: City Cycling Tour
exercises.passage: The City Cycling Tour is a relaxed two-hour ride designed for visitors who want to see everyday life beyond the main tourist streets. The route passes through a public park, a university area, two quiet residential streets, and a small food market. Helmets and bikes are provided, and the guide stops several times for photos and short explanations. Participants should be comfortable riding in light traffic, but the tour avoids busy roads whenever possible. It is not recommended for people who have not ridden a bike recently. In case of heavy rain, the tour is moved to the next morning or fully refunded.

questions.question: What makes the tour different from typical tourist routes?
options: It focuses on everyday city life

questions.question: Participants need to bring their own helmet.
Who should probably avoid this tour?
options: People who can ride in light traffic
People who have not cycled recently

questions.question: The tour organizers seem to care about...

exercises.teaching_note: Ask students to identify risk/safety language.

exercises.title: A Digital Detox Weekend
exercises.passage: When Leo booked a digital detox weekend, he imagined it would be easy to turn off his phone. By Saturday morning, he realized how often he reached for it without thinking. The retreat organizers collected devices at reception and offered paper maps, board games, nature walks, and cooking classes instead. At first, Leo felt disconnected from everything, but after a few hours, he started noticing small things: the sound of rain on the windows, the taste of fresh bread, and the fact that conversations lasted longer when nobody checked messages. On Sunday, he got his phone back. He did not decide to delete every app, but he changed his notification settings before leaving.

questions.question: What is the main idea of the passage?
options: Leo learned that cooking is difficult
Leo discovered how phone habits affected him
Phones should be banned in all hotels

questions.question: Leo immediately found the detox easy.
What did Leo change before leaving?
What can we infer about Leo?
options: He wanted a more balanced use of technology

questions.question: In the text, 'without thinking' means...

exercises.teaching_note: Good transition into lifestyle habits and technology discussion.

exercises.title: The Wednesday Walking Group
exercises.passage: Every Wednesday evening, a group of neighbors meets outside the library for a forty-minute walk. The idea began when a local nurse noticed that many older residents wanted to exercise but felt uncomfortable joining a gym. Now the group includes people of different ages, including students who want a break from screens and parents pushing strollers. The pace is gentle, and nobody is expected to be athletic. After the walk, some members stay for tea at a nearby café. The organizer says the goal is not only fitness, but also connection. For some participants, it is the only regular social event of the week.

questions.question: Why did the walking group begin?
options: A nurse saw a community need

questions.question: Only older residents can join the group.
What is one purpose of the group besides exercise?
The walking group is probably successful because it is...

exercises.teaching_note: Use to discuss community lifestyle and social well-being.

exercises.title: Shopping with Less Waste
exercises.passage: Refill stores are becoming more common in some neighborhoods. Instead of buying rice, soap, or cereal in new packages each time, customers bring their own containers and pay by weight. Supporters say this reduces plastic waste and encourages people to buy only what they need. However, refill shopping also requires planning. Customers must remember clean containers, check prices carefully, and sometimes visit more than one store to find everything. For people with busy schedules, convenience can be a barrier. Still, some families say the habit becomes easier after a few weeks, especially when they keep a shopping kit near the door.

questions.question: What is the main topic of the passage?
options: A way to shop with less packaging

questions.question: Refill shopping may require more planning than regular shopping.
In the text, 'barrier' means...
What practical suggestion is mentioned?
options: Keep a shopping kit near the door

questions.question: The writer presents refill shopping as...

exercises.teaching_note: Ask students to find contrast words: however, still, especially.

exercises.title: Working Remotely from a Smaller City
exercises.passage: After two years of remote work, Ana moved from a capital city to a smaller coastal town. She expected lower rent and quieter mornings, and she found both. However, the change also affected her routine in unexpected ways. There were fewer professional events, and the internet was less reliable during storms. On the other hand, she spent less time commuting and more time cooking, exercising, and meeting neighbors. Ana says the move did not solve every problem, but it made her more aware of how location influences lifestyle. She now visits the capital once a month for meetings and cultural events, while keeping her daily life slower and less expensive.

questions.question: Why did Ana move to a smaller city?
The internet was always reliable in the coastal town.
Which benefit is mentioned?
options: More time for cooking and exercise

questions.question: Ana’s opinion about the move is best described as...
Why does Ana visit the capital monthly?

exercises.teaching_note: Useful for B2 comparison and balanced opinion practice.

listening.section_note: Scripts are ready for teacher recording or TTS generation. Each includes pre-, while-, and post-listening stages.

exercises.title: Hotel Check-In with a Room Request
exercises.script: Receptionist: Good evening. Welcome to River View Hotel. How can I help you? Guest: Hi, I have a reservation under Daniel Costa. Receptionist: Let me check. Yes, one single room for two nights. Guest: That’s right. I also requested a quiet room because I have an online meeting tomorrow morning. Receptionist: I see the note here. We can give you a room on the fifth floor, away from the elevator. Guest: That would be perfect. Is breakfast included? Receptionist: Yes, breakfast is served from seven to ten. The gym opens at six if you want to exercise before your meeting. Guest: Great. I’ll probably use it tomorrow. Receptionist: Here is your key card. Enjoy your stay.
exercises.pre_listening: Ask: What do people usually request when checking in at a hotel?

while_listening.question: How long is Daniel staying?
Why does he want a quiet room?
while_listening.answer: He has an online meeting tomorrow morning.

while_listening.question: What time does the gym open?

exercises.post_listening: Role-play a hotel check-in and make one lifestyle-related request.

exercises.title: Directions to a Healthy Café
exercises.script: Tourist: Excuse me, is there a healthy café near here? Local: Yes, there’s one called Green Corner. It’s about ten minutes away on foot. Tourist: Great. How do I get there? Local: Walk straight along this street until you see the public library. Turn left there, and keep walking for two blocks. The café is next to a bike shop. Tourist: Is it expensive? Local: Not really. They have lunch bowls, smoothies, and soup. It’s popular with students and office workers, so it can get crowded after twelve thirty. Tourist: Maybe I’ll go now, then. Local: Good idea. If you arrive before noon, you’ll probably find a table.
exercises.pre_listening: Students predict café vocabulary: smoothie, bowl, crowded, table.

while_listening.question: How far away is the café on foot?
What is the café next to?
When does it get crowded?

exercises.post_listening: Students give directions from school/home to a healthy place nearby.

exercises.title: Making a Dinner Reservation
exercises.script: Host: Good afternoon, Bella Vista Restaurant. How may I help you? Caller: Hi, I’d like to book a table for Friday evening. Host: Of course. How many people? Caller: Four. We’re celebrating my sister’s new job, so we’d like a quiet table if possible. Host: We have a table at seven or eight thirty. Caller: Seven is better. Do you have vegetarian options? Host: Yes, we have several vegetarian dishes, and we can also prepare meals without dairy if you tell your server. Caller: That’s helpful. One person in our group avoids dairy. Host: No problem. May I have your name and phone number?
exercises.pre_listening: Ask students what information restaurants need for a reservation.

while_listening.question: What day is the reservation for?
How many people will come?
What dietary need is mentioned?

exercises.post_listening: Students create a reservation dialogue with one special request.

exercises.title: Airport Announcement about Delays
exercises.script: Attention passengers traveling on Flight 482 to Lisbon. Due to strong winds, boarding will begin approximately forty minutes later than planned. Please remain near Gate 16 and check the screens for updates. Passengers who need assistance should speak to a member of staff at the service desk. The airport lounge is currently full, but free water is available near the gate. If you have a connecting flight, please do not leave the area. Our team will provide updated information as soon as possible. We apologize for the delay and thank you for your patience.
exercises.pre_listening: Students list common airport announcement words.

while_listening.question: What caused the delay?
How late will boarding begin?
What should passengers with connecting flights do?
while_listening.answer: They should not leave the area.

exercises.post_listening: Students write one short airport announcement.

exercises.title: Travel Agent and Wellness Holiday
exercises.script: Agent: So, what kind of holiday are you looking for? Customer: I want to relax, but I don’t want to just sit by a pool all day. Agent: In that case, a wellness holiday might suit you. There’s a place by the lake with yoga classes, short hikes, and healthy cooking workshops. Customer: That sounds interesting. Is it suitable for beginners? Agent: Definitely. The activities are gentle, and guests can choose what they want to join. Customer: I like that. I also need reliable internet because I may have to check work emails. Agent: The rooms have Wi-Fi, but the program encourages guests to limit screen time.
exercises.pre_listening: Discuss: What makes a holiday relaxing but active?

while_listening.question: What type of holiday does the agent suggest?
Name two activities mentioned.
while_listening.answer: Yoga, short hikes, healthy cooking workshops.

while_listening.question: Why does the customer need internet?

exercises.post_listening: Students recommend a holiday for a busy professional.

exercises.title: Interview: Changing Morning Habits
exercises.script: Interviewer: You changed your morning routine last year. What was the biggest difference? Guest: I stopped checking my phone as soon as I woke up. At first, it felt strange because I was used to reading messages in bed. Interviewer: What do you do instead? Guest: I drink water, stretch for five minutes, and write down the three most important things I need to do. It sounds simple, but it helps me start the day calmly. Interviewer: Did it improve your productivity? Guest: Yes, but not magically. I still have busy days. The main benefit is that I feel more in control before other people’s messages enter my mind.
exercises.pre_listening: Students rank morning habits from healthiest to least healthy.

while_listening.question: What habit did the guest stop doing?
while_listening.answer: Checking the phone immediately after waking up.

while_listening.question: What three things does the guest do now?
while_listening.answer: Drinks water, stretches, writes down three important tasks.

while_listening.question: What is the main benefit?

exercises.post_listening: Students describe one habit they would like to change.

exercises.title: Narration: A Weekend Without Plans
exercises.script: Last month, I decided not to plan my weekend. Usually, I fill every free hour with errands, social events, and exercise classes. This time, I only made one rule: no work emails. On Saturday morning, I walked to a bakery without checking the time. Later, I cleaned my apartment while listening to music and called a friend I had not spoken to in weeks. By Sunday evening, I realized that an unplanned weekend was not lazy. It gave me space to notice what I actually wanted to do. Now I try to leave at least one afternoon free every week.
exercises.pre_listening: Students discuss planned vs unplanned weekends.

while_listening.question: What was the speaker’s only rule?
Who did the speaker call?
while_listening.answer: A friend they had not spoken to in weeks.

while_listening.question: What did the speaker learn?
while_listening.answer: An unplanned weekend can provide space and is not lazy.

exercises.post_listening: Students plan a balanced weekend with free time.

exercises.title: Conversation: Solving a Travel Problem
exercises.script: A: I just checked the train app. Our train has been canceled. B: Seriously? The cooking class starts in an hour. A: I know. There’s a bus, but it takes forty-five minutes and leaves in ten. B: That might work if we walk fast. What about a taxi? A: It’s expensive, and traffic looks bad. The bus lane may actually be quicker. B: Okay, let’s take the bus. Can you message the instructor and say we might arrive a few minutes late? A: Sure. I’ll explain the train was canceled. B: Thanks. At least we planned to leave early, or this would be much worse.
exercises.pre_listening: Students predict transport problems during a trip.

while_listening.question: What happened to the train?
Why might the bus be better than a taxi?
while_listening.answer: The bus lane may be quicker because traffic is bad.

while_listening.question: Who will receive a message?

exercises.post_listening: Students role-play solving a late-arrival problem.

exercises.title: Announcement: Community Fitness Day
exercises.script: Good morning, everyone. Welcome to Community Fitness Day. Activities will begin at nine thirty with a gentle warm-up in the main square. At ten, you can choose between a beginner cycling session, a family walk through the park, or a short workshop on healthy meal planning. Please sign your name at each activity table before joining, as spaces are limited. Water stations are located beside the library and near the playground. If you feel unwell at any time, speak to one of the volunteers wearing blue shirts. Remember, today is not a competition. The goal is to try something new and meet people in your neighborhood.
exercises.pre_listening: Students match activity names to pictures or definitions.

while_listening.question: What time does the warm-up begin?
Why should people sign their names?
What is the goal of the day?
while_listening.answer: To try something new and meet people.

exercises.post_listening: Students design a community lifestyle event.

exercises.title: Interview: Remote Work and Lifestyle
exercises.script: Interviewer: You moved to a smaller city while keeping your remote job. Was it difficult? Guest: In some ways, yes. I had to create clearer boundaries because my home became my office. At first, I worked longer hours because there was no commute to mark the end of the day. Interviewer: How did you solve that? Guest: I started leaving the house for a short walk at six, even if I was not going anywhere special. It became a signal that work was over. Interviewer: Do you miss the capital? Guest: Sometimes. I miss cultural events, but I don’t miss the noise or the rent.
exercises.pre_listening: Students discuss advantages and disadvantages of remote work.

while_listening.question: What problem did the guest have at first?
What does the guest do at six?
What does the guest miss?

exercises.post_listening: Students give advice to a remote worker.

listen_life_01.l1_q2: He has an online meeting tomorrow morning.

listen_life_04.l4_q3: They should not leave the area.

listen_life_05.l5_q2: Yoga, short hikes, healthy cooking workshops.

listen_life_06.l6_q1: Checking the phone immediately after waking up.
listen_life_06.l6_q2: Drinks water, stretches, writes down three important tasks.

listen_life_07.l7_q2: A friend they had not spoken to in weeks.
listen_life_07.l7_q3: An unplanned weekend can provide space and is not lazy.

listen_life_08.l8_q2: The bus lane may be quicker because traffic is bad.

listen_life_09.l9_q3: To try something new and meet people.

speaking.section_note: Ten speaking tasks for individual, pair, and group work. No answer key is required; assess using fluency, coherence, vocabulary, grammar, and pronunciation.

exercises.title: Describe a Hotel Check-In Scene
exercises.setup: Show the hotel reception image from Reading Exercise 1.
exercises.instructions: Describe the picture. Say where the people are, what they are doing, and how they probably feel.
follow_up_questions: Have you ever had a difficult check-in experience?
What makes a hotel stay comfortable?
exercises.teaching_note: Push students beyond listing objects: action + inference + reason.

exercises.title: Recommend a Healthy Place in Your City
exercises.setup: Student A is a visitor; Student B is a local resident.
exercises.instructions: Recommend a café, park, gym, market, or walking route. Explain where it is, why it is good, and who would enjoy it.
discussion_questions: What places in a city support a healthy lifestyle?
target_language: The best thing about it is...
exercises.teaching_note: Useful for real-world recommendation language.

exercises.title: A Weekend Plan for Balance
exercises.setup: Students create a balanced weekend including rest, chores, social time, and exercise.
exercises.instructions: Present your weekend plan and explain why it is balanced.
follow_up_questions: What would you remove if you became too busy?
Do you prefer planned or spontaneous weekends?
exercises.teaching_note: Connect to future forms and lifestyle vocabulary.

exercises.title: Solve a Travel Problem
exercises.setup: Students receive a problem card: canceled train, lost booking, noisy room, or closed restaurant.
exercises.instructions: Discuss the problem, suggest two solutions, choose the best one, and explain your decision.
discussion_questions: What is more important: saving time or saving money?
How can people stay calm when travel plans change?
target_language: I think the best solution is...
exercises.teaching_note: Great for modals, negotiation, and decision-making.

exercises.title: Opinion: Digital Detox
exercises.setup: Students respond to an opinion prompt.
exercises.instructions: Do you think people should spend one day a week without social media? Give reasons and examples.
follow_up_questions: Would this be realistic for students or workers?
What are the disadvantages of being offline?
exercises.teaching_note: Train Statement → Reason → Support.

exercises.title: Compare Two Lifestyles
exercises.setup: Group compares city lifestyle and small-town lifestyle.
exercises.instructions: Compare advantages and disadvantages of living in a big city and a smaller town. Decide which is better for a remote worker.
exercises.teaching_note: Encourage balanced answers rather than one-sided opinions.

exercises.title: Plan a Community Fitness Day
exercises.setup: Groups design a simple event for different ages.
exercises.timing: Preparation: 5 minutes. Presentation: 2 minutes per group.
exercises.instructions: Plan three activities, choose a location, decide what equipment is needed, and explain how you will make the event inclusive.
discussion_questions: How can communities encourage healthier habits?
Should local governments pay for free health events?
exercises.teaching_note: Links community topic with planning language.

exercises.title: Role-Play: Restaurant Special Request
exercises.setup: Student A is a customer with a dietary need; Student B is a restaurant host.
exercises.instructions: Make a reservation and ask about vegetarian, dairy-free, or low-salt options. The host must offer solutions politely.
discussion_questions: How should restaurants deal with special requests?
Is healthy food more popular now than before?
target_language: Let me check that for you.
exercises.teaching_note: Politeness and functional language practice.

exercises.title: Mini Presentation: One Habit That Changed My Life
exercises.setup: Students choose one habit: sleep routine, walking, meal planning, budgeting, reading, or reducing screen time.
exercises.instructions: Explain the habit, why you started it, what was difficult, and what changed.
follow_up_questions: Would you recommend this habit to everyone?
What makes habits difficult to maintain?
exercises.teaching_note: Excellent for past tenses, sequencing, and reflection.

exercises.title: Persuade a Manager to Support Work-Life Balance
exercises.setup: Student speaks to a manager and proposes one lifestyle-friendly workplace change.
exercises.instructions: Persuade a manager to introduce one change: flexible hours, walking meetings, healthier cafeteria options, or no-meeting Friday afternoons. Give reasons and address one possible concern.
follow_up_questions: What concern might the manager have?
How can you make your idea practical?
exercises.teaching_note: This is close to MET persuasive speaking: position + reasons + audience awareness.

writing.section_note: Ten writing tasks progressing from short personal communication to B2 essays and proposals.

exercises.title: Informal Email: A Short Trip
exercises.prompt: Write an email to a friend about a short trip you recently took. Mention where you stayed, what you did, and one thing you would do differently next time.
success_criteria: Include at least one feeling or opinion
exercises.teaching_note: Good first writing task after Reading 1-2.

exercises.title: Restaurant Review
exercises.prompt: Write a review of a café or restaurant that supports a healthy lifestyle. Describe the food, atmosphere, service, and whether you would recommend it.
success_criteria: Mention at least one positive and one negative point
exercises.teaching_note: Connect to food vocabulary and comparative language.

exercises.title: Travel Itinerary
exercises.prompt: Create a one-day itinerary for a visitor who wants a relaxing lifestyle experience in your city. Include morning, afternoon, and evening activities.
exercises.teaching_note: Useful for platform submissions with structured format.

exercises.title: Formal Email: Hotel Problem
exercises.prompt: You stayed at a hotel, but your room was noisy and the Wi-Fi did not work well. Write a formal email to the hotel manager explaining the problem and requesting a solution.
exercises.teaching_note: Practice complaint structure: context → problem → impact → request.

exercises.title: Blog Post: A Digital Detox Weekend
exercises.prompt: Write a blog post about spending a weekend with limited phone use. Explain what you did, how you felt, and whether you would recommend it.
exercises.teaching_note: Encourage linking words: at first, later, by the end.

exercises.title: Opinion Paragraph: Remote Work
exercises.prompt: Some people say remote work improves lifestyle. Others say it makes work-life balance harder. What is your opinion?
exercises.teaching_note: Use Statement → Reason → Support.

exercises.title: Advice Email: Healthy Routine
exercises.prompt: A friend says they feel tired and disorganized because of their routine. Write an email giving practical advice about sleep, food, exercise, or screen time.
exercises.teaching_note: Good for should, could, might want to, have to.

exercises.title: For-and-Against Essay: Living in a Smaller City
exercises.prompt: Discuss the advantages and disadvantages of moving from a large city to a smaller city for a better lifestyle.
exercises.teaching_note: Supports B2 contrast and argument structure.

exercises.title: Proposal: Community Wellness Event
exercises.prompt: Your school or workplace wants to organize a community wellness event. Write a proposal suggesting activities, explaining benefits, and mentioning possible challenges.
exercises.teaching_note: Useful for workplace/community English.

exercises.title: Argumentative Essay: Lifestyle and Technology
exercises.prompt: Technology has made modern life more convenient, but not necessarily healthier. To what extent do you agree?
exercises.teaching_note: Final writing task; assess coherence, paragraphing, and range.

vocabulary.section_note: Ten vocabulary sets with definitions and task types for platform exercises.

exercises.title: Accommodation

target_words.reception: the front desk area in a hotel
target_words.reservation: an arrangement to keep a room or table for you
target_words.key card: a plastic card used to open a hotel room
target_words.elevator: a machine that carries people between floors
target_words.lobby: the entrance area of a hotel
target_words.available: free or ready to be used
target_words.included: part of the price or service

exercises.task: Match each word to its definition.

exercises.title: Healthy Food Choices

target_words.smoothie: a thick drink made with fruit or vegetables
target_words.portion: an amount of food served to one person
target_words.balanced meal: a meal with different food groups
target_words.ingredient: one food item used in a recipe

exercises.task: Complete: 1) This soup is ___ because it has no milk. 2) A ___ meal includes protein, vegetables, and grains. 3) A ___ is often made with fruit and yogurt.

exercises.title: Transportation and Movement

target_words.commute: travel regularly between home and work
target_words.delay: a situation when something happens later than planned
target_words.route: the way from one place to another
target_words.gate: the area where passengers board a plane
target_words.connection: a second bus, train, or flight
target_words.cancel: to decide that something will not happen

exercises.task: Choose the best collocation: 1) heavy ___ 2) flight ___ 3) get a ___ 4) miss a ___

exercises.title: Wellness Activities

target_words.stretching: moving muscles gently to make them less tight
target_words.guided walk: a walk led by someone who explains the place
target_words.retreat: a place or event for rest and personal improvement
target_words.energy: the feeling of being able to do things

exercises.task: Complete: 1) The weekend ___ helped me rest. 2) Good ___ can improve your mornings. 3) Five minutes of ___ can help after sitting too long.

exercises.title: Technology and Habits

target_words.notification: a message or alert from an app
target_words.app: a program on a phone or computer
target_words.digital detox: a period of using technology less
target_words.settings: options that control how a device works

exercises.task: Complete with the correct word: 1) I turned off app ___. 2) A weekend without my phone was a digital ___. 3) I changed my privacy ___.

exercises.title: City and Community

target_words.neighborhood: an area of a town or city
target_words.resident: a person who lives in a place
target_words.community: people living or working together in an area
target_words.inclusive: open and suitable for different people
target_words.volunteer: a person who helps without being paid

exercises.task: Match the words to examples: a person helping at an event, a place everyone can use, people living in the same area, open to different people.

exercises.title: Work-Life Balance

target_words.boundary: a limit between two areas or parts of life
target_words.productivity: how much useful work is done
target_words.burnout: extreme tiredness from stress or work
target_words.remote work: working from a place outside the office
target_words.schedule: a plan of times and activities
target_words.priority: something more important than other things

exercises.task: Complete: 1) Remote workers need clear ___. 2) Too much stress can lead to ___. 3) A daily ___ helps me organize tasks.

exercises.title: Sustainable Lifestyle

target_words.reusable: able to be used many times
target_words.container: something used to hold food or liquid
target_words.by weight: priced according to how heavy it is

exercises.task: Choose the best collocation: 1) reusable ___ 2) reduce ___ 3) buy ___ weight 4) develop a healthy ___

exercises.title: Feelings and Reactions

target_words.relieved: happy because a problem is gone
target_words.overwhelmed: feeling unable to deal with too much
target_words.disconnected: not feeling connected to people or things

exercises.task: Choose: 1) After the hotel changed my room, I felt ___. 2) Too many notifications make me feel ___. 3) After a quiet walk, I felt ___.

exercises.title: B2 Lifestyle Collocations

target_words.make a reservation: book a table, room, or service
target_words.miss a connection: fail to catch the next transport

exercises.task: Complete: 1) I need to ___ a reservation. 2) Remote workers should ___ boundaries. 3) Walking can ___ stress. 4) We may ___ a connection if the train is late.

grammar.section_note: Ten grammar exercises with lifestyle/travel contexts, examples, and answer keys.

exercises.title: Present Simple vs Present Continuous
examples: I usually walk to work.
This week I am staying at a hotel.
task: 1) I usually ___ breakfast at home. (have)
2) This week, she ___ in a rented apartment. (stay)
3) They ___ yoga every Monday. (do)
4) We ___ for a quiet café right now. (look)
5) He often ___ his phone before bed. (check)

exercises.title: Past Simple vs Past Continuous
examples: I was waiting at reception when my friend arrived.
We missed the bus yesterday.
task: 1) I ___ dinner when the hotel called. (have)
2) She ___ her suitcase at the airport. (lose)
3) We ___ to the market when it started raining. (walk)
4) They ___ a great café last weekend. (find)
5) He ___ when the announcement started. (sleep)

exercises.title: Present Perfect for Experiences
examples: I have stayed in many hostels.
She has never tried a digital detox.
task: 1) I ___ never ___ a wellness retreat. (try)
2) She ___ already ___ the hotel. (book)
3) We ___ visited this market before. (not)
4) Have you ever ___ a flight? (miss)
5) They ___ just ___ from their trip. (return)

exercises.title: Future Forms
examples: I’m going to visit the market.
I’ll call the hotel now.
task: 1) We ___ stay near the beach next month. (plan)
2) I think the café ___ be crowded. (prediction)
3) The train is late, so I ___ take a taxi. (decision now)
4) She ___ meeting the guide at 9. (arrangement)
5) They ___ probably enjoy the walking tour. (prediction)

exercises.title: Modals for Advice and Suggestions
examples: You should drink more water.
You could try walking after work.
task: 1) You ___ book early because the hotel is popular.
2) You ___ try a digital detox if notifications stress you.
3) You ___ eat in the Quiet Zone; it is not allowed.
4) We ___ take the bus, but a taxi is faster.
5) You ___ talk to reception if the room is noisy.

exercises.title: Comparatives and Superlatives
examples: This room is quieter than the first one.
The earliest train is the most convenient.
task: 1) A smaller city is usually ___ than a capital. (quiet)
2) This café is ___ than the one near the station. (healthy)
3) The gym is the ___ place in the hotel. (busy)
4) Walking is ___ expensive than taking taxis. (little)
5) This is the ___ routine I have tried. (good)

exercises.title: First Conditional
examples: If the train is late, we will take the bus.
If you arrive early, you will find a table.
task: 1) If the café ___ crowded, we ___ take food away. (be / get)
2) If you ___ before noon, you ___ a quiet table. (arrive / find)
3) If it ___ heavily, the tour ___ canceled. (rain / be)
4) If she ___ her notifications, she ___ calmer. (turn off / feel)
5) If we ___ late, we ___ the instructor. (be / message)

exercises.title: Second Conditional
examples: If I lived near the beach, I would walk every morning.
If I had more time, I would cook more often.
task: 1) If I ___ remotely, I ___ to a smaller city. (work / move)
2) If she ___ more free time, she ___ a walking group. (have / join)
3) If we ___ near a market, we ___ fresh food more often. (live / buy)
4) If he ___ fewer notifications, he ___ less stressed. (receive / feel)
5) If they ___ a car, they ___ weekend trips. (have / take)

exercises.title: Relative Clauses
examples: The café that opened near my office is excellent.
The guide, who was very friendly, explained the route.
task: Combine: 1) The hotel is near the river. We stayed there.
2) The receptionist was helpful. She changed my room.
3) The app sends notifications. It helps me track habits.
4) The market opens on Sundays. It sells local food.
5) The walking group meets outside the library. It is free.

answer_key.1: The hotel where we stayed is near the river.
answer_key.2: The receptionist who changed my room was helpful.
answer_key.3: The app that helps me track habits sends notifications.
answer_key.4: The market that sells local food opens on Sundays.
answer_key.5: The walking group that meets outside the library is free.

exercises.title: Linking Words for B2 Answers
examples: The city is expensive; however, it offers many opportunities.
I turned off notifications because they distracted me.
task: Choose: because / however / therefore / although / in addition. 1) The apartment was small; ___, it was very clean.
2) I booked early ___ prices increase quickly.
3) The train was canceled; ___, we took the bus.
4) ___ the café was crowded, the service was fast.
5) The hotel has a gym. ___, it offers healthy breakfast options.

gram_life_09.1: The hotel where we stayed is near the river.
gram_life_09.2: The receptionist who changed my room was helpful.
gram_life_09.3: The app that helps me track habits sends notifications.
gram_life_09.4: The market that sells local food opens on Sundays.
gram_life_09.5: The walking group that meets outside the library is free.

teaching_notes: Use the pack progressively: Reading/Listening 1-3 for B1+, 4-7 for B1-B2 transition, 8-10 for stronger B2 practice.
For platform use, objective tasks can be auto-corrected. Speaking and writing should be teacher-reviewed or reviewed with a rubric.
For MET-style development, train students to support answers with reason + example, especially in speaking and writing.
Keep student-facing feedback concise: one strength, one focus area, and one next action.
